sentences.text: It was in the fall.
We stayed a few months because my sister wasn't able to walk.
So-- and we didn't have food.
So once we paid her, she was already OK.
She was wearing our coats in the church, you know, the fur coat?
And everybody knew that something, you know, is happening-- why did she got that fur coat?
So we were thinking to leave her.
But, because of my sister-- she was sick.
So I-- I decided to go to our people, what they were-- they were dealing with my mother [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] during-- before the war and ask them for food.
The people-- Ukraine people, but they were dealing with my mother.
And they were OK.
I mean, they didn't want to keep us, no question.
But at least they should offer some food.
So this guy, [?
Adelka's ?] husband, took me once.
He rented-- or I don't know because he was poor himself.
He didn't have a wagon.
He took from somewhere a wagon and-- and horses.
And he put me on the wagon.
And he went with me in another village, you know.
While we was-- we were traveling, you know, a police guy came over and was asking, where is going?
Where-- so he went away from the wagon.
I kept the-- how do you call those, from the horses?
The reins.
Yeah, and I was going on there.
And he came over.
He looked at me.
And he didn't say a thing.
He didn't recognize me, you know?
So it was really a danger when-- when I saw him.
But he didn't recognize.
I was lucky.
So we came to the other village.
And I came over to the guys-- two guys, I remember.
And they gave me some food, bread.
They knew who you were?
Yeah, I told them.
But they didn't want to-- I should stay longer there.
You know, they gave me the food and go.
That's all.
They told me, just, if you go and you will hide yourself, you will through-- get through this.
But you have to hide yourself.
I said, but give me a place where.
He said, go and hide yourself.
Then you'll-- you'll live through this.
That's what their advice was.
But at least they didn't want to bother too much.
They gave me the food.
We came back home to the-- [?
Adelka. ?] And we had-- believe it or not, we were sharing with them because they were poor.
So we were sharing with them the food, too.
But then the Ukraine people-- the police, I mean, and the German-- started to go on the Polish people.
They-- they wanted to chase out the Polish from the-- from the villages, Polish nationality.
Anyway, so then, it was for us, a relief because the Polish were already on the same level like the Jews.
You know?
So it was for us a relief.
So the Polish started to pack their things.
We didn't have much to pack, naturally.
But they had to pack their things.
And we helped them, naturally.
And we were going away.
After, there was a river Sluch.
I remember the name Sluch, a big river.
So we went to this river.
And it was like-- how do you call this, what goes on the river, like in Victoria, a-- a 'terry'?
A ferry.
A ferry!
A little boat that takes you across the river.
Exactly.
It's not a boat actually, at that time.
It was something not so professionally made.
But we just decided to go.
We had to wait because it was a lot of Polish people and a few Jewish people, what they were hiding themself.
So-- so we went through the ferry to the forest, to the real big forest.
In those big forests, we met some other people, Jewish people, a family [?
Edelman, ?] [?
Edelman. ?] And this family took us in.
And they had a place in the forest made special, too, for them.
And they were like, it was an open house, you know, whoever came hiding themselves, they came to this family.
And we were with-- with-- there.
It was tight.
But we had to have food.
So some people from there, they were stealing at night.
They were going into the gardens.
And they were stealing potatoes, tomatoes, or things like that.
Me, with my sister, I couldn't do this.
But because I knew how to sew a little bit and handcraft and things like that, we were going to the houses asking for work.
Because we were far already from the German, not so close after the river, you know?
Across the river, I mean.
So we were asking for work.
So I did all kind, my sister, too.
We were babysitting.
I was sewing.
I was cleaning up.
I was-- I don't know how to call this.
And they had the animals in the field.
I had-- I used to watch them.
In our time, the Jews, they used to say, [YIDDISH].
You know-- you know what it means?
So-- so all kind of work.
Did these people know that you were Jewish?
Yeah, they knew.
Mostly they were Polish, Polish people, yeah.
In one house, we were-- I was sewing there for them.
But they had a small house.
And they had a big-- how to call this, when you go to camp, you-- you-- the-- they used to take big--
Oh, the knapsack, come on.
At that time, a knapsack?
It was a big box, what it kept their clothes, a big-- big, big heavy box.
A trunk, exactly.
But the trunk was not like today the trunks.
It was a huge trunk.
And the trunk was moved away from the wall.
So between the wall and the trunk, I was sitting with my sister the whole day.
I couldn't breathe.
I didn't have to-- to cough sometimes, no.
Because people were coming in, neighbors.
They shouldn't notice that somebody's there.
And this was our hiding place.
Just in the evening, we used to go out because we had to.
You know.
In the evening?
To go-- like, you know, you-- you're only a person.
You have to go to the washroom.
Or-- you know?
So you just, you had to attend those business in the evening, not during the day.
But they feed us.
The children not with-- I mean, their food.
We were happy to have this.
And I used to sew for them, remodel things, and-- make flowers for the-- for the lapels for their coat, for, you know, all kinds of things.
Yeah.
In the-- they had near the houses, they had for the animals.
You know?
Barns, that's right.
So we slept in the barns on the hay.
No, they didn't keep us to sleep in the house.
They didn't have place, too.
You know.
And they were afraid.
Once--
No.
I-- I knew one name.
Once I had-- we had a bad experience, I mean, a very big-- big scare.
Because being in a house, very nice people.
And I used to sew for them.
It's a different family.
From one family to the other, I used to go with my sister.
But the other family had a big house already.
But what happened once, all of a sudden the German-- the German surrounded this place, the forest.
And the guy from the house, the owner, left the house.
He didn't want to stay.
He was afraid.
And his daughter, I remember, went away and left us both, me with my sister in the house.
The German came in and started to ask me if I have a-- if Jewish people are here.
I made myself so that I don't understand what he's talking in German.
I don't understand.
Then he told me he needs a hammer because he had a nail in his boot.
He needs a hammer to put away the nail.
I couldn't understand him.
My sister was keeping-- how do I call this, a necklace-- they are-- they have necklaces with a cross.
You know, it's a religious symbol, I think, by them.
So she was keeping the necklace.
And I was keeping the Bible at the time to show them that we are-- but we weren't dressed like, elegant.
You know, we were dressed like schleppers, like, you know, like them.
So-- and the German left us.
But the way we felt afterwards is just-- you know, we were shaking.
While they were around, we-- we were calm.
But when they left, we didn't find the place, you know?
So scared we were.
When they left-- and I started-- and the man came back, and the daughter-in-law came back, and-- all over again.
And then the-- and I started to work.
I had to sew for them.
We never forget this.
He gave me a piece of material to-- to make a pair of pants.
I had the pattern from old one, naturally, because I didn't know how to make the pants.
From old pants, I put on this material and started.
But what did I do?
Both legs-- I made-- I-- the material I put together so both legs ran [LAUGHS] together.
You know?
I didn't know what-- how to do something.
My-- I was mixed up completely.
We never forget.
I didn't know how to fix afterwards because I had to put in material.
It's-- it's a different story when you make two legs separate.
I make both pieces of material for one leg.
That was the-- the time when I hid there.
They knew.
That's why they left the house.
They knew that the Germans are coming, so they left the house for us.
They didn't care about the house.
They didn't care about us.
They cared about their life that time, you know, in case they will recognize us and they are the owners.
So they will kill them, too.
So that--
Yeah, yeah.
I was staying there.
Then they gave me another family, I think.
I went somewhere else.
She had even a bigger house.
So I went in the back room.
And I was doing work.
But mostly like, for the weekend, for Shabbos, for, you know, I-- I came to the forest because we had food, you know?
I didn't work for money at the time, I had for bread.
So-- When you rejoined these people in the forest, what type of living accommodation did they have?
They were hiding themself.
And they were sharing with everybody.
No, underground.
It was everything underground and topped with grass and with-- you know.
And they were under.
They called it [NON-ENGLISH] at the time.
It's a Jewish word-- or I don't know what kind of word it is.
But they called it [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH].
We were sleeping like, herring in a-- in a barn.
That's what it was.
But they had a-- some things they baked, I remember, bread.
They baked themselves bread.
It was like, you know, pita and thin.
And we cooked potatoes, for instance.
I brought their potatoes from-- from the-- from my work, potatoes in rusted-- rusted cans.
You know, where did they get those cans, rusted cans?
We cooked with the peel.
And the water was yellow.
And-- but we were strong-- no peels, nothing, no temperature.
What did you hear from the outside world?
Did you have any communication at all--
No.
No, no.
When I was working by those people-- so they used to come from the city.
And they used to tell us that no Jewish people, everybody's gone, no.
They used to tell us and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] nowhere to come.
And we were waiting, waiting till-- till something-- a miracle will happen.
From the partisan, mostly.
Close by were partisans, too.
We didn't belong to them because they were-- they could abuse, you know, special girls.
So we didn't want to-- to be with them.
How did I know?
Once we were by a Polish lady.
I was working for her, too.
And she had a boyfriend, a partisan.
And we were sleeping there once because I knew that the partisan is the boyfriend.
And, you know, I was [? saved ?.] So we're just the same.
One evening, at night, I mean, they were-- partisan were banging in the door.
They were looking for girls.
So we were lucky that this lady had this boyfriend.
And he chased them away.
You know?
So that's why I didn't want to belong, my sister, too.
But we were-- [? close, ?] that when we needed some [YIDDISH], what kind of clothes actually?
So we had-- but mostly I remember the knees were always like frozen like ice.
You know?
It was terrible.
Did you have any knowledge of what the partisans were doing?
Yeah, oh yeah.
They were doing a lot.
They were helping.
In one-- I mean, they were against the-- the German.
And the German had the Ukraine, too.
They were worse than the German, the Ukraine.
And they had a party-- they called them a special partisan party from the Ukraine.
And they helped the German to look in the forest to find Jews in there.
So-- and those partisans were against those Ukraine, against those German.
You know?
So they were mostly with us.
So some Jewish people belonged to the partisans, oh, yeah.
And some not.
And they helped, you know?
They got-- for instance, my friend-- one of my friend, she got-- she got injured.
She was in the partisan.
I think she's still alive in Israel.
Anyway, so she got injured.
And they came.
And they took her away to Moscow.
The partisans, they had connection with Russian.
And they took her away during the war.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
Yep.
Yes, but when I was across the river already.
But before, I didn't do those things.
Before I was running away from Korzec.
So I couldn't do those things.
[INTERPOSING VOICES]
[LAUGHS] I'm asking the same question myself.
You know?
You know, it's a miracle, probably, not to be sick during the years.
Be-- to be hungry and not to starve, and-- and to be in danger, you know, most of the time, every single day, actually.
You know?
I-- I had the knife in here.
Anybody could kill me.
You know?
So just to be in danger, it's-- Is there any one incident that stands out most in your mind from those two years?
As I told you, I have a lot.
[PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] No special one.
But, that's--
Which ones, the Jewish people in the forest?
No, the-- the Polish farmers, the different homes that you stayed in.
No, they demand that I should work for them.
I-- I couldn't take, just-- just beg them for food.
I had to-- to-- to do something for them.
And I tried, tried all kind of work.
I remember it was a lady.
She was-- she had a stroke or something was wrong with her.
She wouldn't walk.
So we were like a nurse to her, helping her.
And she liked us.
And she gave us food for this.
We weren't choosy for work.
I-- we did whatever is possible.
And during that time, you always had work.
And you always had a home--
We had--
We had-- we had always, most of the time, yes.
They were glad to keep us and to do.
But we were in hiding.
Because one neighbor didn't know about the other neighbor if I'm there.
You know?
We came just at night.
And we left at night.
Nobody should see us.
No, no.
I have this-- around-- they surrounded us.
And-- and we avoided, we avoided to be close to them, I mean, close to the police.
Or-- or-- it's just when it was necessary already, then I went to-- to ask for food.
And I met the police.
Otherwise, we were avoiding them.
That's why we were in a big forest.
Nobody should see us.
Nobody should hear about us.
Yeah.
Yeah.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But it was not from our town.
It was a-- a boy alone.
And we were close, I mean, you know, friends, very good friends.
And-- But when the war, in '44-- when the war ended by us, actually, the Russian came.
And he was going to come to our town to-- to meet me.
He was killed when the Russian were there.
Because he was going by train.
It was a little train.
I don't know how you call those.
And it was dynamite underneath.
They put dynamite underneath the Ukraine.
And it blew up.
And he was killed.
Living through the war, suffering, and when he was free-- you know, so he was killed.
Goleb, ?] [?
Yossi ?] [?
Goleb. ?] And when I find out this, being in Korzec first, I didn't want to stay there.
I wanted to go away.
And that's it.
Didn't want meet no one, anyone, anything.
I was heartbroken.
Yeah, approximately.
Everything.
We came back to Korzec.
We were staying together, a few families-- families, singles.
We were together in one house close to the police, not far because that time, even, were people killed from the Ukraine people.
They were so mad because we came back.
And we witnessed everything.
We were living witnesses.
You know?
So they were trying to kill us, the Ukraine.
So some people were killed.
And we were staying in one house across the police station.
You know?
And we were very careful not to get in touch with them.
And my-- the lady-- what she gave the fur coats to this guy.
And the lady told us, don't stay by me.
Don't stay here because they will kill you just the same.
Go with your people and close to the police and be protected there.
Yeah.
So then, we were staying there for quite a while.
My sister got married in this house in '44.
And we left Rowne.
We went away.
I didn't want to stay there anymore.
How did you feel when you went back to Korzec, to your home?
I was afraid to be there.
I didn't have-- the house was ruined completely.
But we had just this lady, what she had our things.
She gave us back a few things.
No, I don't remember.
They called her [NON-ENGLISH].
I don't remember.
It's-- it's-- but she was-- she was intelligent woman.
She wasn't so stupid, so brainwashed like that, the Ukraine.
So she gave the rest of things to us back, like pieces of material and, you know, what my mother prepared, you know, for us.
She gave everything back.
She didn't want anything from us.
And we started, you know, in Rowne.
We had some tailors-- what they made up coats for us, dresses, and things like that, to be presented, more or less.
What made you decide to go to Rowne?
It's the-- the-- the closest place.
It's a bigger city.
